item.title:
ONLY 22% OF WORKERS FEEL JOBS ARE SAFE
A global survey by ADP finds just 22% of workers are confident their jobs won’t be eliminated, highlighting widespread insecurity.
Lower-paid and lower-level employees feel the least secure, while executives show higher confidence. Workers who feel secure are far more engaged, productive, and less likely to quit.
AI use appears to help: frequent users report higher confidence and engagement than non-users.
Despite low unemployment, anxiety remains high—62% of workers put in unpaid hours weekly, and many still feel overworked yet uncertain about their future.
Older workers are the least confident in their career prospects, reinforcing concerns about skills and advancement.
item.description:
ONLY 22% OF WORKERS FEEL JOBS ARE SAFE

A global survey by ADP finds just 22% of workers are confident their jobs won’t be eliminated, highlighting widespread insecurity.

Lower-paid and lower-level employees feel the least secure, while executives show higher confidence. Workers who feel secure are far more engaged, productive, and less likely to quit.

AI use appears to help: frequent users report higher confidence and engagement than non-users.

Despite low unemployment, anxiety remains high—62% of workers put in unpaid hours weekly, and many still feel overworked yet uncertain about their future.

Older workers are the least confident in their career prospects, reinforcing concerns about skills and advancement.

item.title: 🚨 IRAN REJECTS CEASEFIRE, WON'T NEGOTIATE WITH PARTY THAT VIOLATED COVENANTS - FARS, CITING SOURCE
item.description: 🚨 IRAN REJECTS CEASEFIRE, WON'T NEGOTIATE WITH PARTY THAT VIOLATED COVENANTS - FARS, CITING SOURCE

item.title:
$100 OIL WON’T HIT LIKE THE 1970s
UBS says today’s global economy is far less vulnerable to oil shocks than in the 1970s, despite rising prices.
Economist Arend Kapteyn highlights that oil spending as a share of GDP has more than halved since 1974 due to improved energy efficiency and economic growth.
In the U.S., oil spending dropped from about 4.8% of GDP in 1974 to ~1.7% today—and would only reach ~2% even if prices hit $100 per barrel. Europe shows a similar decline.
Bottom line: although supply disruptions may rival past crises, the global economy’s lower “oil intensity” means far less macroeconomic strain.
item.description:
$100 OIL WON’T HIT LIKE THE 1970s

UBS says today’s global economy is far less vulnerable to oil shocks than in the 1970s, despite rising prices.

Economist Arend Kapteyn highlights that oil spending as a share of GDP has more than halved since 1974 due to improved energy efficiency and economic growth.

In the U.S., oil spending dropped from about 4.8% of GDP in 1974 to ~1.7% today—and would only reach ~2% even if prices hit $100 per barrel. Europe shows a similar decline.

Bottom line: although supply disruptions may rival past crises, the global economy’s lower “oil intensity” means far less macroeconomic strain.

item.description: SPOT SILVER RISES NEARLY 3% TO $74.55/OZ

item.title:
59% OF AMERICANS SAY IRAN WAR HAS 'GONE TOO FAR'
A new AP-NORC poll shows 59% of Americans believe U.S. military action against Iran has gone too far, raising political risks for Donald Trump.
Public concern is also shifting to the economy: 45% worry about affording gas, up sharply from earlier polling.
While most Americans support preventing Iran from getting nuclear weapons, they equally prioritize keeping fuel prices low—creating a difficult balance for policymakers.
Trump’s approval ratings remain steady at around 40%, but trust in his handling of military decisions is low, and most Americans oppose escalating the conflict, especially sending ground troops.
Partisan divides persist: Democrats overwhelmingly say the action has gone too far, Republicans are split, and both parties strongly agree on one issue—keeping gas prices down.
item.description:
59% OF AMERICANS SAY IRAN WAR HAS 'GONE TOO FAR'

A new AP-NORC poll shows 59% of Americans believe U.S. military action against Iran has gone too far, raising political risks for Donald Trump.

Public concern is also shifting to the economy: 45% worry about affording gas, up sharply from earlier polling.

While most Americans support preventing Iran from getting nuclear weapons, they equally prioritize keeping fuel prices low—creating a difficult balance for policymakers.

Trump’s approval ratings remain steady at around 40%, but trust in his handling of military decisions is low, and most Americans oppose escalating the conflict, especially sending ground troops.

Partisan divides persist: Democrats overwhelmingly say the action has gone too far, Republicans are split, and both parties strongly agree on one issue—keeping gas prices down.

item.title:
IRAN TIGHTENS CONTROL OVER HORMUZ SHIPPING
Iran is demanding detailed crew, cargo, and voyage information from ships seeking safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz, with approvals handled by the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.
The process is inconsistent and sometimes includes fees—mainly for high-value vessels like oil and gas tankers—signaling Tehran’s growing grip on the key trade route.
Traffic remains limited, mostly involving Iranian- or China-linked ships hugging Iran’s coast. While Iran says the strait is still open, non-compliant vessels have been turned away.
Tensions persist despite U.S. pressure, including moves by Donald Trump to push for reopening. The disruption is hitting Asian economies hard, especially India, which faces fuel shortages, while China—a key buyer of Iranian oil—has questioned the legitimacy of transit fees.
item.description:
IRAN TIGHTENS CONTROL OVER HORMUZ SHIPPING

Iran is demanding detailed crew, cargo, and voyage information from ships seeking safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz, with approvals handled by the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.

The process is inconsistent and sometimes includes fees—mainly for high-value vessels like oil and gas tankers—signaling Tehran’s growing grip on the key trade route.

Traffic remains limited, mostly involving Iranian- or China-linked ships hugging Iran’s coast. While Iran says the strait is still open, non-compliant vessels have been turned away.

Tensions persist despite U.S. pressure, including moves by Donald Trump to push for reopening. The disruption is hitting Asian economies hard, especially India, which faces fuel shortages, while China—a key buyer of Iranian oil—has questioned the legitimacy of transit fees.

item.title: ISRAELI MILITARY: A SHORT WHILE AGO SEVERAL WAVES OF STRIKES WERE CARRIED OUT ON TEHRAN
item.description: ISRAELI MILITARY: A SHORT WHILE AGO SEVERAL WAVES OF STRIKES WERE CARRIED OUT ON TEHRAN

item.title:
IRAN WARNS U.S. AS TROOP BUILDUP INTENSIFIES
Iran says it is “closely monitoring” U.S. military movements, warning Washington not to test its resolve. Parliamentary speaker Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf signaled Tehran is not ready for negotiations.
Meanwhile, the Pentagon is preparing to deploy 3,000 troops from the 82nd Airborne Division to the Middle East, though no final decision on ground deployment has been made.
The U.S. is also sending warships and thousands of Marines, with reinforcements expected as early as Friday—coinciding with Donald Trump’s deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Around 50,000 U.S. troops are already engaged in the region.
item.description:
IRAN WARNS U.S. AS TROOP BUILDUP INTENSIFIES

Iran says it is “closely monitoring” U.S. military movements, warning Washington not to test its resolve. Parliamentary speaker Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf signaled Tehran is not ready for negotiations.

Meanwhile, the Pentagon is preparing to deploy 3,000 troops from the 82nd Airborne Division to the Middle East, though no final decision on ground deployment has been made.

The U.S. is also sending warships and thousands of Marines, with reinforcements expected as early as Friday—coinciding with Donald Trump’s deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Around 50,000 U.S. troops are already engaged in the region.

item.title:
SPACEX IPO COULD BREAK RECORDS
SpaceX may file for an IPO as early as this week, according to The Information, aiming to raise $75B+ with a potential June listing.
The move is highly anticipated, especially after its merger with xAI, valuing the combined business at $1.25T. Some estimates suggest it could target a $1.75T valuation.
item.description:
SPACEX IPO COULD BREAK RECORDS

SpaceX may file for an IPO as early as this week, according to The Information, aiming to raise $75B+ with a potential June listing.

The move is highly anticipated, especially after its merger with xAI, valuing the combined business at $1.25T. Some estimates suggest it could target a $1.75T valuation.

item.title: - U.S. AND A GROUP OF REGIONAL MEDIATORS ARE DISCUSSING THE POSSIBILITY OF HOLDING HIGH LEVEL PEACE TALKS WITH IRAN - AXIOS
item.description: - U.S. AND A GROUP OF REGIONAL MEDIATORS ARE DISCUSSING THE POSSIBILITY OF HOLDING HIGH LEVEL PEACE TALKS WITH IRAN - AXIOS

item.title:
U.S. AWAITS IRAN'S RESPONSE ON PEACE SUMMIT AS ISRAEL WATCHES WARILY
The U.S. is waiting on Iran’s response to a possible peace summit as early as Thursday, with mediators involved—but it’s unclear if talks will happen.
Donald Trump wants to wind down the war, but uncertainty remains high. Israel, led by Benjamin Netanyahu, fears a deal that falls short and limits its military options.
Washington says Iran may accept parts of a 15-point plan, including nuclear limits, but there’s no proof and Tehran denies active negotiations.
Meanwhile, the U.S. is preparing for escalation—including troop deployments—while exploring diplomacy. Officials expect at least 2–3 more weeks of fighting as both sides weigh their next move.
item.description:
U.S. AWAITS IRAN'S RESPONSE ON PEACE SUMMIT AS ISRAEL WATCHES WARILY

The U.S. is waiting on Iran’s response to a possible peace summit as early as Thursday, with mediators involved—but it’s unclear if talks will happen.

Donald Trump wants to wind down the war, but uncertainty remains high. Israel, led by Benjamin Netanyahu, fears a deal that falls short and limits its military options.

Washington says Iran may accept parts of a 15-point plan, including nuclear limits, but there’s no proof and Tehran denies active negotiations.

Meanwhile, the U.S. is preparing for escalation—including troop deployments—while exploring diplomacy. Officials expect at least 2–3 more weeks of fighting as both sides weigh their next move.

item.title:
KUWAIT OIL RECOVERY COULD TAKE MONTHS
If the war ended today, Kuwait would need 3–4 months to restore oil production to full capacity, according to KPC’s CEO, as reported by Amena Bakr of Kpler.
item.description:
KUWAIT OIL RECOVERY COULD TAKE MONTHS

If the war ended today, Kuwait would need 3–4 months to restore oil production to full capacity, according to KPC’s CEO, as reported by Amena Bakr of Kpler.

item.title:
82ND AIRBORNE HEADS TO MIDDLE EAST
Fox News has learned that Maj. Gen. Brandon Tegtmeier, commander of the 82nd Airborne Division, and his headquarters staff have been ordered to deploy to the Middle East. The move comes as the Pentagon and White House consider sending the division for possible land operations, including potential missions in Iran such as Kharg Island.
The New York Times first reported yesterday that the Pentagon is weighing this deployment.
item.description:
82ND AIRBORNE HEADS TO MIDDLE EAST

Fox News has learned that Maj. Gen. Brandon Tegtmeier, commander of the 82nd Airborne Division, and his headquarters staff have been ordered to deploy to the Middle East. The move comes as the Pentagon and White House consider sending the division for possible land operations, including potential missions in Iran such as Kharg Island.

The New York Times first reported yesterday that the Pentagon is weighing this deployment.

item.title:
IRAN WORRIES TALKS MIGHT BE A TRAP
Officials in Tehran are concerned Trump’s postponement of strikes on energy infrastructure could be an attempt to damp oil prices before resuming
item.description:
IRAN WORRIES TALKS MIGHT BE A TRAP

Officials in Tehran are concerned Trump’s postponement of strikes on energy infrastructure could be an attempt to damp oil prices before resuming

item.title:
IRAN TURNS BACK CONTAINER SHIP IN STRAIT OF HORMUZ
The container ship Selen was stopped for lacking proper permission to transit the Strait of Hormuz, according to IRGC Navy commander Alireza Tangsiri. Passage requires full coordination with Iran’s maritime authorities.
item.description:
IRAN TURNS BACK CONTAINER SHIP IN STRAIT OF HORMUZ

The container ship Selen was stopped for lacking proper permission to transit the Strait of Hormuz, according to IRGC Navy commander Alireza Tangsiri. Passage requires full coordination with Iran’s maritime authorities.

item.title:
SHELL CEO WARNS IRAN WAR COULD HIT FUEL SUPPLIES
Shell CEO Wael Sawan says the Iran conflict is tightening fuel supplies, with jet fuel already affected, diesel next, and gasoline at risk ahead of the summer driving season.
Europe could see shortages by April, and South and Northeast Asia are already feeling pressure. Shell is advising governments on demand cuts, stockpile use, and alternative purchases.
A recent Iranian missile strike also hit Shell’s Pearl facility in Qatar, though the site remains secure with no injuries.
item.description:
SHELL CEO WARNS IRAN WAR COULD HIT FUEL SUPPLIES

Shell CEO Wael Sawan says the Iran conflict is tightening fuel supplies, with jet fuel already affected, diesel next, and gasoline at risk ahead of the summer driving season.

Europe could see shortages by April, and South and Northeast Asia are already feeling pressure. Shell is advising governments on demand cuts, stockpile use, and alternative purchases.

A recent Iranian missile strike also hit Shell’s Pearl facility in Qatar, though the site remains secure with no injuries.

item.title:
KALSHI HITS RECORD MARCH MADNESS VOLUME AMID NCAA PUSHBACK
Kalshi’s prediction markets are booming during March Madness, with over $800M traded in the first weekend—nearly double last year’s total—and $1B offered for a perfect bracket. Total basketball volume on Kalshi now tops $1.6B, outpacing football.
item.description:
KALSHI HITS RECORD MARCH MADNESS VOLUME AMID NCAA PUSHBACK

Kalshi’s prediction markets are booming during March Madness, with over $800M traded in the first weekend—nearly double last year’s total—and $1B offered for a perfect bracket. Total basketball volume on Kalshi now tops $1.6B, outpacing football.
